thread.title: US Senator Ron Wyden Talks Blazers, Walton, Sharpe
thread.title_full: US Senator Ron Wyden Talks Blazers, Walton, Sharpe

author: Conor Bergin
title: US Senator Ron Wyden Talks Blazers, Walton, Sharpe
text:
Long before he became a United States senator, Oregon’s Senator Ron Wyden had a dream very relatable to those who grew up shooting hoops: wanting to play professional basketball.
Friday morning, Wyden talked with Blazer’s Edge editors Brandon Goldner and Conor Bergin of the “We Like the Blazers” podcast about those hoop dreams, the Portland Trail Blazers, the need for the franchise to erect a statue of Bill Walton and Maurice Lucas, the nuclear athleticism of Shaedon Sharpe, and more.
“I grew up for years and years believing that I could play in the NBA,” Wyden said.
Moving all over the Midwest and ultimately out West when he was young, basketball was more than an outlet for Wyden’s fandom: It was social connection, and a way for a shy kid to gain confidence.
“We lived very close by a junior high school, and there was a basketball on the side of the gym, and I just picked it up and started shooting,” he said. “I shot and shot and shot for literally hours on end.”
Hear
[the full interview with Senator Ron Wyden]on the We Like the Blazers podcast.
Like most of us, he didn’t quite make it to the NBA. Unlike most of us, however, the 6-foot-4 Wyden did make it to college on a basketball scholarship at the University of California at Santa Barbara. He also is in the Congressional record for his free-throw shooting prowess.
“We had a free throw shooting contest in the House... I was walking through with my son and it was proctored and they just kept track of it, and I made 47 out of 50 shots,” Wyden said.
While the story was confirmed by another congressperson during a committee hearing and is part of the official record, Wyden doesn’t want to have to repeat his performance:
“Now I live in fear that someday I’m going to get brought into a charitable contest, and I’ll shoot a bunch of bricks, and they’ll call for a big investigation.”
Wyden has been a Blazers fan for many years: He’s not only someone who experienced firsthand the Blazers’ first and only championship in 1977, but he got to rub elbows with some of the biggest names on that legendary team, including the late great Bill Walton and Maurice Lucas. Wyden later became close to Walton, who would urge him to “be more liberal” when they would see each other at the airport. Walton would be flying frequently for NBA broadcasting duties and Wyden for his work in the House, and later the Senate.
Wyden is now a leading voice calling for the Blazers to memorialize both Walton and Lucas outside of the Moda Center. Following Walton’s passing in the spring, Wyden wrote a guest column in The Oregonian advocating for the franchise to erect a statue honoring the bond the teammates shared on and off floor. He voiced his support for that cause again in Friday’s interview.
“In a time when there’s so much polarization... Mo Lucas and Bill Walton would be a perfect message [to send to] the community and the state right now,” Wyden said.
Wyden has also been a leading voice supporting the Albina Vision Trust to rebuild the lower Albina District near the Moda Canter, a predominantly Black neighborhood that was destroyed by urban renewal policies and the construction and continued presence of Interstate 5. The Blazers recently partnered with the nonprofit to form the Albina Rose Alliance, and Wyden recently supported over $450 million in funding to cap I-5 and reconnect neighborhoods separated by it.
“If we do this right, this is going to be a crown jewel for Portland going forward in the years ahead because we know that wrongs were done, the community didn’t get a fair shake,” Wyden said. “Now what we’re doing is talking about trying to focus on the kind of essentials that are going to make this a spectacular place, not just as a historical memory, but a place right now where families can get quality, affordable housing, good-paying jobs, access to healthcare and essential services.”
Over the course of the interview, Wyden shared a lot of happy memories about the Blazers players and organizational members who helped bring the team its lone title decades ago. Looking toward the future, the senator sees a lot of promise in third-year Blazers guard Shaedon Sharpe. Wyden called Sharpe the “straightforward call” for his favorite Blazer on the current roster.
“What a talent and what a quality guy, really low-key, team-oriented,” Wyden said of Sharpe. “I think he’s got a chance to be up there in the Blazer Superstar history. ... In one of the opening games, he stuffed on [Minnesota Timberwolves forward] Julius Randle... Randle didn’t even try to go up with Shae.”
Wyden wasn’t too proud to admit that despite having NBA dreams as a kid, Sharpe might have him beat on athleticism.
“I never had that problem where people were afraid to go up with me.”
We Like the Blazers is a weekly podcast hosted by Blazer’s Edge’s Brandon Goldner and Conor Bergin. You can find our work on any pod catcher, on the Blazer’s Edge YouTube feed, or at WeLikeTheBlazers.com.
